revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sendai Hadakamairi]][[ja:どんと祭における裸参り]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q97185865]]
Wikidata name: Sendai hadaka-mairi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:裸参り1.jpg|thumb|Naked Pilgrimage on the approach to [[:en:Ōsaki Hachimangū|Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine]] during the Matsutaki Festival]]
[[File:仙台裸参り 大崎八幡宮松焚祭裸参り.png|thumb|386x386px|Naked Pilgrimage at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine during heavy snow in 2013]]
'''Sendai Naked Pilgrimage''' (仙台裸参り, Sendai Hadaka-mairi) is a traditional event held in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]]. Taking place during the coldest time of the year (around January), participants dressed in loincloths, sandals, and other minimal clothing walk to shrines to pray. Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Bibliography ==

* {{Cite book|author=仙台市教育委員会|title=大崎八幡宮の松焚祭りと裸参り|series=仙台市文化財調査報告書305|date=2006-12|publisher=仙台市教育委員会}}

== Related Articles ==

Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

== References ==

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[d:" to "[[da:d:"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sendai Hadakamairi]][[ja:どんと祭における裸参り]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97185865]]
Wikidata name: Sendai hadaka-mairi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:裸参り1.jpg|thumb|Naked Pilgrimage on the approach to [[:en:Ōsaki Hachimangū|Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine]] during the Matsutaki Festival]]
[[File:仙台裸参り 大崎八幡宮松焚祭裸参り.png|thumb|386x386px|Naked Pilgrimage at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine during heavy snow in 2013]]
'''Sendai Naked Pilgrimage''' (仙台裸参り, Sendai Hadaka-mairi) is a traditional event held in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]]. Taking place during the coldest time of the year (around January), participants dressed in loincloths, sandals, and other minimal clothing walk to shrines to pray. Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sendai Hadakamairi]][[ja:どんと祭における裸参り]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97185865]]
Wikidata name: Sendai hadaka-mairi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:裸参り1.jpg|thumb|Naked Pilgrimage on the approach to [[:en:Ōsaki Hachimangū|Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine]] during the Matsutaki Festival]]
[[File:仙台裸参り 大崎八幡宮松焚祭裸参り.png|thumb|386x386px|Naked Pilgrimage at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine during heavy snow in 2013]]
'''Sendai Naked Pilgrimage''' (仙台裸参り, Sendai Hadaka-mairi) is a traditional event held in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]]. Taking place during the coldest time of the year (around January), participants dressed in loincloths, sandals, and other minimal clothing walk to shrines to pray. Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

Wikidata name: Sendai hadaka-mairi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:裸参り1.jpg|thumb|Naked Pilgrimage on the approach to [[:en:Ōsaki Hachimangū|Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine]] during the Matsutaki Festival]]
[[File:仙台裸参り 大崎八幡宮松焚祭裸参り.png|thumb|386x386px|Naked Pilgrimage at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine during heavy snow in 2013]]
'''Sendai Naked Pilgrimage''' (仙台裸参り, Sendai Hadaka-mairi) is a traditional event held in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]]. Taking place during the coldest time of the year (around January), participants dressed in loincloths, sandals, and other minimal clothing walk to shrines to pray. Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

* {{Cite book|author=仙台市教育委員会|title=大崎八幡宮の松焚祭りと裸参り|series=仙台市文化財調査報告書305|date=2006-12|publisher=仙台市教育委員会}}

In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

[[File:裸参り1.jpg|thumb|Naked Pilgrimage on the approach to [[:en:Ōsaki Hachimangū|Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine]] during the Matsutaki Festival]]
[[File:仙台裸参り 大崎八幡宮松焚祭裸参り.png|thumb|386x386px|Naked Pilgrimage at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine during heavy snow in 2013]]
'''Sendai Naked Pilgrimage''' (仙台裸参り, Sendai Hadaka-mairi) is a traditional event held in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]]. Taking place during the coldest time of the year (around January), participants dressed in loincloths, sandals, and other minimal clothing walk to shrines to pray. Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sendai Hadakamairi]][[ja:どんと祭における裸参り]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97185865]]
Wikidata name: Sendai hadaka-mairi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:裸参り1.jpg|thumb|Naked Pilgrimage on the approach to [[:en:Ōsaki Hachimangū|Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine]] during the Matsutaki Festival]]
[[File:仙台裸参り 大崎八幡宮松焚祭裸参り.png|thumb|386x386px|Naked Pilgrimage at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine during heavy snow in 2013]]
'''Sendai Naked Pilgrimage''' (仙台裸参り, Sendai Hadaka-mairi) is a traditional event held in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]]. Taking place during the coldest time of the year (around January), participants dressed in loincloths, sandals, and other minimal clothing walk to shrines to pray. Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sendai Hadakamairi]][[ja:どんと祭における裸参り]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sendai Hadakamairi]][[ja:どんと祭における裸参り]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sendai Hadakamairi]][[ja:どんと祭における裸参り]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97185865]]
Wikidata name: Sendai hadaka-mairi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:裸参り1.jpg|thumb|Naked Pilgrimage on the approach to [[:en:Ōsaki Hachimangū|Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine]] during the Matsutaki Festival]]
[[File:仙台裸参り 大崎八幡宮松焚祭裸参り.png|thumb|386x386px|Naked Pilgrimage at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine during heavy snow in 2013]]
'''Sendai Naked Pilgrimage''' (仙台裸参り, Sendai Hadaka-mairi) is a traditional event held in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]]. Taking place during the coldest time of the year (around January), participants dressed in loincloths, sandals, and other minimal clothing walk to shrines to pray. Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper=[[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]]|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like [[:en:Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō]], its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|author=仙台市教育委員会|title=天賞酒造に係る文化財調査報告書|series=仙台市文化財調査報告書304|date=2006-11|publisher=仙台市教育委員会}}
* {{Cite book|author=仙台市教育委員会|title=大崎八幡宮の松焚祭りと裸参り|series=仙台市文化財調査報告書305|date=2006-12|publisher=仙台市教育委員会}}

== Related Articles ==
* [[:en:Hadaka Matsuri|Naked Festivals]]

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper={{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|WD=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun}}|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:仙台年中行事絵巻.jpg|thumb|386x386px|An excerpt from "Sendai Annual Events Scroll" (嘉永 era, 1848-1854), depicting "New Year Customs"]]
The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to {{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka}} eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like {{ill|Sagichō Fire Festival|en|Sagichō Fire Festival|WD=Q9332740|lt=Sagichō}}, its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

The earliest reference to a naked pilgrimage around Sendai appears in the book *Sendaishi Genten* (仙臺始源), written during the {{ill|An’ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An’ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193910}} to {{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka|1=Culture}} eras (late 18th to early 19th century). It describes devotees visiting the Kinokita Yakushi temple wearing thin clothes during harsh winter conditions, referred to as "Naked Pilgrimage" (裸参り).{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=22}}

The 1849 book *Ōu Ichiran Dōchū Hizakurige* mentions crowds visiting Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine on January 14-15 to burn pine decorations.<ref>{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}</ref> In the 1850s, the *Sendai Annual Events Scroll* depicts men in loincloths carrying bells and buckets, inscribed with "Sugawara," believed to be a sake brewery in Sendai. Researchers suggest that the practice began with sake brewers praying for brewing safety and expanded into a local tradition.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-12}}

== Matsutaki Festival ==
The {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine is a central Shinto ritual where shrine staff burn pine decorations, while residents participate in traditional practices like naked pilgrimages. Although the festival has similarities to other events like {{ill|Sagichō Fire Festival|en|Sagichō Fire Festival|WD=Q9332740|lt=Sagichō|1=Sagichō Fire Festival}}, its prevalence in Miyagi suggests its origin at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

The naked pilgrimage emphasizes the sacred transformation of participants, elevating them as revered visitors (marebito). It has become an indispensable part of the Matsutaki Festival.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=11-13}}

== Connection to Sake Brewing ==
Men depicted in the 1850s naked pilgrimage scroll were likely involved in sake brewing. According to local lore, brewers from Sendai’s Sugawara Brewery began participating to pray for safe brewing. In 2006, Sendai City Educational Committee confirmed that sake brewers played a crucial role in formalizing the pilgrimage.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=9}}

Sake brewers like {{ill|Tenshō Shuzō|ja|天賞酒造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Shuzō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were particularly involved, providing tools for the pilgrimage and influencing its rituals. Brewer participation declined in the late 20th century, and by 2004, Tenshō Shuzō ceased participating due to relocation and operational changes. A citizens' group, the Sendai Traditional Naked Pilgrimage Preservation Society, formed in 2006 to uphold traditional practices.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|pp=47}}

== Changes in Naked Pilgrimage ==
The traditional pilgrimage methods once strictly adhered to by sake brewers have shifted to include diverse groups. While traditional rituals emphasize purification, silence, and strict roles, many contemporary participants use the event for promotion or community engagement. Preservation groups continue to pass on the original sake brewer traditions.{{Sfn|仙台市教育委員会|2006b|p=47}}

[[File:裸参り1.jpg|thumb|Naked Pilgrimage on the approach to {{ill|Ōsaki Hachimangū|en|Ōsaki Hachimangū|WD=Q874428|lt=Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine|1=Ōsaki Hachimangū}} during the Matsutaki Festival]]
[[File:仙台裸参り 大崎八幡宮松焚祭裸参り.png|thumb|386x386px|Naked Pilgrimage at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine during heavy snow in 2013]]
'''Sendai Naked Pilgrimage''' (仙台裸参り, Sendai Hadaka-mairi) is a traditional event held in {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai|1=Sendai}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}. Taking place during the coldest time of the year (around January), participants dressed in loincloths, sandals, and other minimal clothing walk to shrines to pray. Closely associated with the {{ill|Matsutaki Festival|ja|松焚祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsutaki Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine and the {{ill|Donto Festival|ja|どんと祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Donto Festival|qq=}} celebrated throughout Miyagi, it has spread to other areas in the prefecture.

The origins of the pilgrimage are linked to {{ill|Nambu Tōji|ja|南部杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nambu Tōji|qq=}}, traditional sake brewers (explained below). Over time, participants have expanded beyond brewers to include citizens, employees of companies, universities, hospitals, teachers, care workers, and various organizations. Participation peaked in 1992 with 200 groups and 7,500 participants but has since stabilized at around 100 groups and 3,000 participants annually. In 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, participation at Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine’s Matsutaki Festival dropped to 50 groups and 368 participants, with some areas canceling the event entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210114/k00/00m/040/288000c|title=コロナ終息願い「どんと祭」　「裸参り」参加者は激減　仙台・大崎八幡宮|newspaper={{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|WD=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|1=Mainichi Shimbun}}|date=2021-01-15|accessdate=2021-01-23}}</ref>
